coredata.dc:title: Construction of bifunctional carbon dots based fluorescent/colorimetric/smartphone-assisted multi-signal strategy for monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shun
Han, Beibei
Chen, Mengshuo
Han, Yaqing
Liu, Jifa
Wang, Guannan
coredata.dc:description: In view of the importance of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as a clinical diagnostic indicator of related diseases and physiological research, developing a reliable measurement assay with high efficiency and accuracy is of tremendous value. In this study, bifunctional carbon dots (CDs) with peroxidase-like and fluorescence properties were synthesized through a readily available solvothermal method. Peroxidase-like CDs could oxidaze colorless 1,4-phenylenediamine (PPD) to the colored oxidized product (oxPPD), which quenched the CDs' fluorescence. A multi-signal sensing system was strategically fabricated for detecting ALP for the first time, taking advantage of CDs' remarkable peroxidase-mimic activity and fluorescence properties. The limits of detection (LOD) and linear ranges for ALP was 0.034 and 0.027 U/L, 0.1 to 1.8 and 0.05 to 1.8 U/L by the colorimetric and fluorescent method, respectively. Besides, the results of hydrogel devices processed by smartphones also exhibited satisfactory results. The multi-signal strategy developed in this study provides a new impetus for designing different biosensors with bifunctional nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Peroxidase-mimic
Fluorescence
Alkaline phosphatase
Multi-signal strategy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of CDs for colorimetric and fluorescent analysis of ALP
Fabrication of portable kit for ALP sensing
Integration of the fluorescence and peroxidase-like property of CDs
ALP system construction based on PPi-regulated catalytic inhibition
ALP analysis in human serum samples

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs. (b) DLS result and (c) XRD pattern of CDs. (d) UV–vis absorption, FL excitation, and emission spectra of CDs. (e) FL spectra of CDs solution under varied excitation wavelengths. (f) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) C 1 s peak, (h) N 1 s peak, and (i) O 1 s peak of CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of PPD, PPD/H2O2, CDs/H2O2, CDs/PPD, CDs/PPD/H2O2. (b) EPR spectra of blank, H2O2, CDs/H2O2, and CDs/H2O2/PPi system.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs, CDs/H2O2, CDs/PPD, CDs/PPD/H2O2. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, oxPPD, CDs/oxPPD, and the sum of the spectra of CDs and oxPPD. (c) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs, and absorbance spectrum of oxPPD. (d) Fluorescence lifetimes of CDs in the different amounts of oxPPD.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) absorbance intensity at 530 nm, (c) FL emission spectra, and (d) FL emission intensity at 485 nm of CDs/PPD/H2O2 system in the presence of 15 μM PPi, 30 μM ATP, ADP, AMP, and anions.

(a) UV–vis absorption and (c) FL emission spectra of CDs/PPD/H2O2 in the presence of varied amounts of PPi. (b) Relationship between A0-A at 530 nm and PPi concentration. (d) A plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) at 485 nm versus the ALP concentration. (e) UV–vis absorption and (f) FL emission spectra of CDs/PPD/H2O2 system with blank, ALP, PPi, and ALP/PPi, respectively.

(a) UV–vis absorption and (c) fluorescence spectra of the sensing system with different ALP activities. (b) Absorbance value at 485 nm and (d) fluorescence intensity at 530 nm versus various ALP activities.
(a) Operation steps for the analysis of ALP by the designed portable hydrogel test kit, (b) Screen captures by HUAWEI P40, and (c) linear relationship of B/S versus ALP concentration (0.1–2.0 U/L).

(a) UV–vis and (c) fluorescence responses of the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system with ALP or the different interfering substances. (b) UV–vis and (d) fluorescence responses of the ALP/PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system with the interfering substances. 1.8 U/L ALP. 50 μM ions, amino acids, glucose, and choline, 5 μg/mL HSA and cholesterol, 9.0 U/L of other biological enzymes.
Schematic illustration for the bifunctional carbon dots nanozyme-based multi-signal system to monitor ALP activity.

head.ce:title: Construction of bifunctional carbon dots based fluorescent/colorimetric/smartphone-assisted multi-signal strategy for monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Medical Engineering & The Key Laboratory for Medical Functional Nanomaterials, Jining Medical University, Jining 272067, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Medical Engineering & The Key Laboratory for Medical Functional Nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Medical Engineering & The Key Laboratory for Medical Functional Nanomaterials, Jining Medical University, Jining 272067, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Hand and Foot Surgery, The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Hand and Foot Surgery
The First Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Hand and Foot Surgery, The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130031, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In view of the importance of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as a clinical diagnostic indicator of related diseases and physiological research, developing a reliable measurement assay with high efficiency and accuracy is of tremendous value. In this study, bifunctional carbon dots (CDs) with peroxidase-like and fluorescence properties were synthesized through a readily available solvothermal method. Peroxidase-like CDs could oxidaze colorless 1,4-phenylenediamine (PPD) to the colored oxidized product (oxPPD), which quenched the CDs' fluorescence. A multi-signal sensing system was strategically fabricated for detecting ALP for the first time, taking advantage of CDs' remarkable peroxidase-mimic activity and fluorescence properties. The limits of detection (LOD) and linear ranges for ALP was 0.034 and 0.027 U/L, 0.1 to 1.8 and 0.05 to 1.8 U/L by the colorimetric and fluorescent method, respectively. Besides, the results of hydrogel devices processed by smartphones also exhibited satisfactory results. The multi-signal strategy developed in this study provides a new impetus for designing different biosensors with bifunctional nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a phosphomonoester hydrolase enzyme ubiquitous in mammalian tissues and fluids [1,2]. It is extensively used as a biomarker in enzyme immunoassays and molecular biology [3]. Additionally, ALP is one of the most commonly monitored enzymes in the conventional clinical application [4]. Typically, the ALP content in the adult serum ranges from 40 to 150 U/L [5]. However, increasing evidence from research investigations indicates that the aberrant expression of ALP may cause malignant diseases [6,7]. For instance, the elevated serum ALP level is often associated with bone cancer and hepatobiliary diseases [8]. In contrast, patients with chronic nephritis and leukemia usually display a decreased serum ALP content [9]. Therefore, ALP is widely used as a pivotal clinical auxiliary diagnostic index and an effective prognostic indicator. Thus, reliable measurement of ALP level is extremely valuable for providing effective approaches to disease diagnosis and physiological research.

Recently, fluorimetry and colorimetry-based methods have emerged for ALP analysis. These methods have the advantages of robust analysis, cheap apparatus, simple operation, direct visualization in the colorimetric method, high-throughput capability, and short response time in fluorimetry [10–12]. However, most of these monitoring methods are based on the output of a single signal, inhibiting the monitoring ALP activity with high accuracy due to the complex disturbances in real samples. Conversely, based on the integration of multi-mode signal, the multiple sensing method can integrate the advantages of every sensing mode and eliminate fluctuations through the self-calibration of the multi signals. It is therefore desirable and meaningful to design multi-signal strategy for the reliable, convenient and low-cost determination of ALP activity.

There has been a significant increase in research on applications of CDs in recent years in sensing, catalysis, therapy, and electronics [13,14]. Due to their intrinsic fluorescent properties, CDs can act as fluorescent probes for monitoring analytes in biological systems by enhancing or quenching their fluorescent signal [15,16]. Besides, CDs can also be utilized as nanozymes due to their excellent catalytic activity. Since the first report on the peroxidase activity of CDs, oxidizing TMB resulting in a chromogenic reaction [17], various types of mimetic CDs, especially metal-doped carbon dots, have been utilized in biomedicine [18,19]. It is rarely reported that fluorescence and mimetic enzyme are used simultaneously in one work. The ingenious combination of fluorescence and enzyme-like properties will achieve satisfying result in the sensing field.

Herein, bifunctional CDs with peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence properties were prepared from galactose and L-histidine precursors in a green preparation route. The prepared fluorescent CDs exhibited a peroxidase-mimic activity, oxidizing colorless PPD to generate the reddish-brown colored oxPPD in the presence of H2O2. As a result of the inner filter effect between oxPPD and CDs, the former effectively quenched the CDs’ fluorescence. Notably, the addition of pyrophosphate (PPi) inhibited the peroxidase-mimic activity of CDs, which reduced the generation of colored oxPPD, leading to the fluorescence recovery of CDs in the CDs/PPD/H2O2 catalytic system. PPi can be hydrolyzed by ALP, which caused fluorescence quenching and solution color deepening. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , a sensitive multi-signal (colorimetric/fluorescent/smartphone) sensing approach for ALP was constructed based on a reaction system consisting of PPD, H2O2, and CDs. The LOD for colorimetric and fluorometric signals were calculated as 0.034 and 0.027 U/L, respectively. A simple portable hydrogel-based kit was constructed for the on-site and visual detection of ALP using a smartphone. Compared with the single-mode sensing approach, the analysis results of each mode of the multi-signal strategy can be compared with each other. Besides, one of the modes could be flexibly selected according to the actual demands.

Typically, 0.0360 g galactose and 0.3724 g L-histidine were dissolved in 20 mL H2O containing 0.1 M NaOH. Then the mixed solution was left to reflux at 80 °C with stirring. After 24 h, the solution was cooled down to room temperature and dialyzed against water through a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff 1000) for 24 h. Subsequently, the purified CDs solution was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs for colorimetric and fluorescent analysis of ALP
ce:section.ce:para: For the ALP assay, 15 μL PPi (1 mM), 10 μL ALP of varied concentrations, and 25 μL Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH 8.0) were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Subsequently, 175 μL PPD (10 mM), 350 μL CDs (1 mg/mL), 70 μL H2O2 (50 mM), and 355 μL NAc-HAc buffer solution (100 mM, pH 6.0) was sequentially added, and the mixtures were incubated at 50 °C. After incubating for 35 min, the solution's fluorescence and UV absorbance was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of portable kit for ALP sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, agarose (40 mg) was completely dissolved in boiling water (1.5 mL) with stirring. Then 500 μL CDs (3 mg/mL), 375 μL PPD (20 mM) and 625 μL NAc-HAc (100 mM, pH 6.0) buffer solution was added when the solution was cooled down to 45 °C. After blending, 150 μL of the mixed solution was rapidly dropped to the snap cap of 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tubes, and then cooled and solidified. The prepared agarose gel was stored at 4 °C for further use. For ALP detection, the above process for ALP-catalyzed the hydrolysis of PPi (2.2 section) was performed again. Then the reaction solution and 15 μL H2O2 (50 mM) was added into the agarose gel. The optical image was obtained by smartphone and analyzed with Color Collection app for the analysis of ALP.

The TEM images were collected to reveal CDs' morphological and dimensional characteristics (Fig. 1 a). The average diameter of CDs was around 5.7 ± 0.57 nm, with a homogeneous distribution. Additionally, distinct crystalline lattice fringes were observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, belonging to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphite [20,21]. The results of Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (See Fig. 1b) showed the average hydrodynamic diameter (HDD) of CDs was around 14.3 nm. Due to the hydration and swelling of the particles in water, the TEM diameter of CDs was smaller than HDD. A diffraction peak centered at about 23° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1c) suggested that CDs exhibited a certain crystallinity.

The CDs’ optical characteristics were systematically investigated. The absorption spectrum (See Fig. 1d) revealed a peak in the 250–350 nm region, attributing to the n → π* and π → π* transitions [22,23]. This peak was absent in the absorption spectra of galactose and histidine. Fig. 1d further revealed that the maximum emission wavelength and the maximum excitation wavelength of CDs were 485 nm and 410 nm, respectively. Fig. 1d (insert) showed that the CDs exhibited light yellow in solution under daylight and blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. Fig. 1e exhibited the FL spectra of CDs solution under different excitation wavelengths. The emission peak remained at 460 nm in the 320–360 nm excitation wavelength range. In contrast, when the excitation wavelength was above 360 nm, the CDs exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent FL behavior. The emission peak exhibited a shift from 460 to 520 nm with an increase of excitation wavelength from 360 to 450 nm. The observed excitation-dependent FL behavior was related to the different surface states of the CDs [24].

The structural groups of CDs were identified by the FT-IR (Fig. 1f), broad absorption at 3100–3500 cm−1 were attributed to the N-H and O-H stretching vibrations. The peak located at approximately 2800–3000 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-C and C-H. The strong bands at 1600 and 1398 cm−1 described the bending vibration of the NH3 + and the stretching vibrations of the COO–, respectively. Next, the CDs were analyzed by XPS to evaluate their elemental composition. The XPS spectrum of CDs in Fig. S2 indicated that CDs consisted of C, N, and O elements with binding energy at 284.3, 400.2, and 531.5 eV, respectively. High-resolution XPS of C 1 s (Fig. 1g) revealed three peaks centered at 284.6, 285.9, and 287.8 eV, assigned to C–C/C = C, C–N/C–O, and C = O/C = N groups, respectively. The N 1 s signal was described by three peaks around 398.5, 399.6, and 400.4 eV (Fig. 1h) due to the presence of C-N-C, C = N-C, and N-H, respectively. Meanwhile, the O 1 s spectra shown in Fig. 1i consisted of two peaks at approximately 531.0 and 532.0 eV, which was attributed to the C = O and C-OH/C-O-C groups, respectively. Thus, the XPS results were consistent with the FT-IR results and indicated abundant hydrophilic –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 groups on the CDs surface, enhancing the water solubility of CDs.

Moreover, physical and chemical stability are crucial to ensuring practical applications of the prepared CDs. As revealed in Fig. S3a-d, the CDs retained satisfactory stability under various chemical and physical conditions, enhancing the possibility of biological applications of CDs.

A typical peroxidase substrate 1,4-phenylenediamine (PPD) was chosen as the chromogenic substrate to verify the peroxidase-like catalytic activity. The colorless PPD can be catalytically oxidized to chromogenic oxPPD (5-diamino-N,N′-bis(p-aminophenyl)-l,4-benzoquinone, Fig. S4), which has a distinctive reddish-brown color in acidic conditions. As revealed in Fig. 2
a, the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system turned reddish-brown in the presence of H2O2 with a prominent absorption at 530 nm, indicating the generation of oxPPD. In contrast, the individual PPD, PPD/H2O2, CDs/H2O2, and CDs/PPD systems did not result in any absorbance or color changes. Thus, the results confirmed the intrinsic peroxidase-mimicking activity of CDs.

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was carried out to identify the generated active intermediate during the catalytic reaction to better elucidate the catalytic process displayed in Fig. 2a. Using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the •OH trapping agent, the presence of •OH (Fig. 2b) were observed in the CDs/H2O2 system in comparison with blank and H2O2 system (•OH, marked as solid stars), indicating the generation of •OH during the CDs-regulated catalytic reaction [25]. However, no signal indicating the presence of •OH was observed for the blank or H2O2 systems. The several non-negligible peaks between the four characteristic peaks of •OH were attributed to the oxidized DMPO radicals [26]. Briefly, in the peroxidase-like catalytic process, CDs catalyzed the H2O2 to produce •OH, which effectively oxidized PPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration of the fluorescence and peroxidase-like property of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-based multi-signal sensing system was further explored by integrating the fluorescence and peroxidase-like property of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, individual PPD and H2O2 did not affect the CDs’ fluorescence. However, in the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system, the fluorescence decreased due to the production of oxPPD. The underlying mechanism of the generated oxPPD induced fluorescence quenching of the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system was investigated. There was no difference between the absorption spectrum of the oxPPD/CDs system and the direct sum of the absorption spectra of oxPPD and CDs (Fig. 3b). This result implied no interaction or complex formation between CDs and oxPPD, which excluded the possibility of static quenching. As shown in Fig. 3c, there was a precise overlap between the absorption spectrum of oxPPD and the FL spectra of CDs. This overlap implied that oxPPD could effectively capture the excitation and emission light of CDs. The fluorescent quenching might have been caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [27,28]. Typically, the fluorescence lifetimes of the donors are shortened by the quenchers in the FRET system [29]. However, there was almost no change in the fluorescence lifetime of CDs on adding various amounts of oxPPD (See Fig. 3d and Table S1), ruling out a FRET system. Thus, IFE was accepted as the cause for the generated oxPPD in the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system to trigger the fluorescence quenching of CDs. These results provided the theoretical basis for constructing bifunctional CDs based on a multi-signal sensing strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP system construction based on PPi-regulated catalytic inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: It has been previously reported that the specific target binding toward the surface of nanozyme influences the conversion of H2O2 to •OH radicals [30,31]. Further, the carboxylic and amino groups on the CDs surface facilitate the electron transfer processes to produce •OH [32]. PPi directly interacts with the carboxylic/amino groups exposed on the CDs, interrupting coherent electron transfer for generating •OH. Besides, the PPi may inhibit the diffusion of the catalytic substrate PPD to the CDs surface because of the steric hindrance caused by the PPi on the CDs surface, resulting in the inhibition of the catalytic oxidation of PPD by CDs. As revealed in Fig. 2b, the generation of •OH was significantly inhibited in the presence of PPi, proving the effective catalytic inhibition of CDs by PPi. The PPi inhibited the peroxidase-mimic activity of CDs, which reduced the generation of colored oxPPD. Meanwhile, the oxPPD triggered fluorescence quenching of CDs in the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system was inhibited, resulting in the fluorescence increase of the system. The PPi-regulated inhibition of the catalytic activity of CDs, which caused the absorbance decrease and fluorescence increase of the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system, was further verified by examining the absorbance and fluorescence of the system. As revealed in Fig. 4 , the presence of 15 μM PPi resulted in a sharp absorption decrease at 530 nm of the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system by 80.2% and was accompanied by a 3.3-times increase in fluorescence at 485 nm. Meanwhile, as revealed in the inserts of Fig. 4b and Fig. 4d, a noticeable reddish-brown color fading was observed with the naked eye in the presence of PPi. The system also showed blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light radiation.

To further check the uniqueness of the PPi-inhibited peroxidase-like activity of CDs, the responses of this system to the typical species such as polyphosphate-containing biomolecules (ATP, ADP), phosphate-containing biomolecule (AMP), phosphate-related anions (PO4 3-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -) and other common anions (SO4 2-, CO3 2–, CH3COO– and NO3 –) were systematically explored. First, the effect of the common polyphosphate-containing molecules (ATP and ADP) on the CDs’ catalytic activity was explored. In the presence of 30 μM ADP or ATP, the absorbance of the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system slightly decreased, while the FL emission intensity increased (See Fig. 4). The results suggested that ADP/ATP exhibited a weaker inhibition effect on CDs compared with PPi since the adenosine residue in ADP and ATP sterically inhibited the interaction between the PPi and CDs. Meanwhile, 30 μM phosphate-related anions (PO4 3-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -) and other common anions (SO4 2-, CO3 2–, CH3COO– and NO3 –) did not affect the absorbance and FL emission intensity of the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system (Fig. 4). There was also no obvious color change of the system under the natural light and 365 nm UV light radiation (inserts in Fig. 4b, d). The results suggested that only PPi exhibited efficient and specific inhibition of the catalytic activity of CDs. Therefore, the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system could be a convenient tool for PPi-related target biosensing.

Based on the above mechanism, the bifunctional CDs-based sensing platform was applied for PPi detection. The results suggested that the recognition performance of PPi was related to the sensitivity of the ALP assay. The absorbance and emission spectra of PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system in the existence of a varied amount of PPi were collected under optimized conditions (See Fig. S5 a-f). As shown in Fig. 5a, the colorimetric signal exhibited a PPi-dependent decrease behavior. In contrast, the fluorescent signal displayed a PPi-dependent enhancement behavior (Fig. 5c). Meanwhile, the colors of the detection solutions turned light brown from reddish-brown (Fig. 5a insert). Further, Fig. 5c insert shows that the solution color varied from dark to blue under the 365 nm UV light radiation with increasing PPi amount. Fig. 5b illustrates a good linear relationship between the absorbance difference value at 530 nm (A0–A, where A0 and A are the absorbances at 530 nm of the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system in the absence and presence of PPi) and the PPi concentration in the range 0.1–––1.0 μM. The fitted linear equation could be expressed as A0–A = 0.0185 + 0.272CPPi, R2 = 0.998. Fig. 5d shows that the FL intensity ratio (I/I0, where I0 and I are the absorbances at 530 nm of the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system in the absence and presence of PPi) has a good linear relationship with the PPi in the range of 0.07–––1.0 μM (I/I0 = 0.988 + 1.539CPPi (μM), R2 = 0.992). The limits of detection for fluorometric and colorimetric modes were as low as 21.2 nM and 66.7 nM (3σ/slope rule). Thus, the results demonstrate that the CDs/PPD/H2O2 system could be used for precise and sensitive PPi monitoring, laying the foundation for developing a bifunctional CDs-based multi-signal strategy for ALP analysis.

PPi, a natural substrate for ALP, can be catalytically hydrolyzed by the ALP into two PO4 3- (Pi). Pi exhibited no effect on the peroxidase-mimic activity of CDs (Fig. 4). The feasibility of this bifunctional CDs-based platform for monitoring ALP was assessed using PPi as substrate. Fig. S6 showed negligible fluorescence change of CDs in the presence of PPi, Pi, ALP, or ALP/PPi, guaranteeing the selective fluorescence response of CDs to oxPPD. As revealed in Fig. 5e, the absorbance of the CDs/PPD/H2O2 decreased in the existence of PPi. Meanwhile, ALP did not influence the CDs-regulated catalytic oxidation of PPD (Fig. 5e). However, after the catalytic hydrolysis of PPi by ALP, the absorbance of CDs/PPD/H2O2 was remarkably recovered with the re-appearance of the reddish-brown color (Fig. 5e insert). Similarly, compared with the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system, there was a substantial decrease in the fluorescence emission of ALP/PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system (See Fig. 5f) accompanied by fading of the blue fluorescence (Fig. 5f insert). Thus, the results indicated that the catalytic hydrolysis of PPi by ALP effectively negated the PPi-regulated catalytic inhibition of CDs and revealed a feasible sensing principle of the constructed CDs-based sensing system for ALP analysis.

Under the optimized enzymatic conditions for ALP (Fig. S7a-c), the proposed sensing system for ALP detection was examined by recording the absorbance and fluorescence spectra of PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system in the presence of varying amounts of ALP. The data in Fig. 6
a revealed that the absorbance intensity at 530 nm increased continuously with the increase of ALP content from 0 to 4.0 U/L. Fig. 6a insert shows that the detection solution turned from light to obvious reddish-brown color with an increase in ALP content. The difference of absorbance intensity at 530 nm (AALP–A0, where A0 and AALP were the absorbances at 530 nm of the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 in the absence and presence of ALP) was linearly dependent on the ALP concentration ranging from 0.1 to 1.8 U/L (Fig. 6b). The corresponding work curve was expressed as, AALP-A0 = –0.0490 + 0.255CALP, U/L, R2 = 0.989.

In contrast, the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm decreased with increasing ALP concentration. Fig. 6c insert exhibited that the blue fluorescence gradually faded with the increasing concentrations of ALP under 365 nm UV light radiation. Fig. 6d shows a good linear relationship between the F/F0 (F0 and F was the fluorescence at 530 nm of the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system in the absence and presence of ALP) and ALP concentration ranging from 0.05 to 1.8 U/L. The regression equation was expressed as F/F0 = 1.036 – 0.412CALP (U/L) with R2 of 0.993. The calculated LOD for these colorimetric and fluorometric signals were calculated to be as low as 0.034 and 0.027 U/L based on the 3σ/slope rule. The LODs were lower than the ALP content of adult serum (40 to 150 U/L), which endowed this method with the ability of low ALP activity detection, and the advantage of less real sample consumption.

Inspired by reports on miniaturized optical sensor [33,34], a portable and instrument-free hydrogels test kit was designed for the convenient detection of ALP with the assistance of a smartphone. This idea was based on the obvious visual color change associated with ALP detection (Fig. 7a). A color identification app (Color Collection) installed on a smartphone was applied to acquire a color digital signal. Under optimal colorimetric conditions, different concentrations of ALP were added to the CDs-based hydrogels. The digital color values such as hue (H), saturation (S), and brightness (B) were obtained after analyzing photos by the app. The photos captured by smartphone under visible light were investigated. The hydrogel colors varied from light to dark with the increase in ALP concentration (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig. 7c, the ratios of saturation to brightness (B/S) value showed an impeccable linear relationship with the ALP concentration (0.1–2.0 U/L). The fitted equation was B/S = 3.1582 – 0.8128CALP (U/L) (R2 = 0.955). With acceptable sensitivity and stability, the CDs-based portable kit was suitable for ALP detection, holding great potential for smart-home monitoring. The smartphone-based detection relies on portable sensors to obtain analysis results in shorter time, solving the limitations of traditional large testing instruments that cannot be monitored in real time [35,36].

Selective recognition ability was one of the key parameters in assessing the potential applications of the designed strategy in complex biomedical detection. Hence, the selectivity of the sensor for ALP analysis was evaluated by using the common substances in biological samples, especially the reductive substances and biological enzymes. Fig. 8 a, c demonstrated that only 1.8 U/L ALP prompted an obvious absorbance and fluorescence change of the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system. The controls biological enzymes (lysozyme (LZM), adenosine deaminase (ADA), trypsin (TRY), protein kinase A (PKA), α-glucosidase (α-GC), glutathione-S-transferase (GST), tyrosinase (TYR), thrombin (THR), and amylase (Amy)) showed feeble variations of absorbance and fluorescence. Meanwhile, other biological components (ions, amino acids and other biomolecules) showed negligible influences on the absorbance and fluorescence of the sensing system. The platform's absorbance and fluorescence signal responses to 1.8 U/L ALP in the presence of the abovementioned controls were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 8b, d, the same signal responses of the ALP/PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system were observed with the controls. Besides, the reducing biomolecules could consume the •OH, inhibiting the catalytic activity of CDs. The common interference from the reducing biomolecules in the biological samples, including ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), and cysteine (Cys) on the CDs/PPD/H2O2 and ALP/PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system was systematically investigated. The results illustrated in Fig. S8 and S9 revealed that the effects of Cys and GSH can be averted by adding thiol-blocking reagents (N-ethylmaleimide, NEM). Further, AA has been reported to be depleted by oxidation during the treatment of biological samples [37]. The results indicated this strategy possessed superior selectivity to resist interferences from common co-existing substances.

Compared with other methods for ALP activity analysis, the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system exhibited several apparent advantages. (1) As summarized in Table S2, the LOD was lower than many reported methods, implying the high sensitivity of this method towards ALP detection. (2) Compared with the reported catalyst/fluorescent probe based on colorimetry and fluorescence dual-mode strategy, the PPi/CDs/PPD/H2O2 system integrated the peroxidase-like and fluorescence property of CDs, avoiding using extra catalyzers or fluorescent substances. (3) The developed method included a multi-signal detection approach, which combined with UV–visible absorption, fluorescence, and smartphone-enabled sensing system to make full use of the advantages of these three modes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP analysis in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The ALP activity has been reported to be a biomarker in the human serum for some clinical diseases [38], therefore, human serum was selected as the model matrix for testing the clinical application of this ALP detection assay. Since the average ALP levels in the serum of healthy adults are 40–150 U/L [5], the human samples were finally 200-fold diluted to ensure that the original ALP level of test samples was in the linear range of this approach. Besides, human samples were pre-treated by NEM to eliminate the interferences of bio-thiols (Cys and GSH) in the serum samples. Besides, ALP was also detected by the standard PNPP-based assay for comparison with the assay developed in this study (Fig. S10). As listed in Table S3 and Fig. S11, the original ALP activity in serum sample obtained by the colorimetric, fluorescent, and smartphone mode was consistent with the PNPP-based method and the reported values of previous work. These results implied this strategy possessed great potential for quantifying ALP activity in real samples with outstanding accuracy and reliability.

The performance of the prepared CDs with peroxidase and fluorescence for the ALP detection was compared with several reported optical sensors. The sensing platforms based on nano-enzyme materials typically require a certain incubation time to ensure that the substrate can be catalyzed thoroughly [39,40]. Surprisingly, it is worth mentioning that the LOD of colorimetry and fluorescence is lower in comparison with other optical sensors based on various nanomaterials, such as manganese dioxide nanosheets [41], MoS2 quantum dots [42], Fe–N–C single-atom nanozymes [43], and more information can be found in Table S2. The extremely low detection concentration makes it easier to sensitively recognize ALP in human serum. As seen (Table S2), the linear range is narrow, therefore, samples with higher concentrations need to be diluted for detection. In short, this work has promising prospects in ALP detection in terms of practicability, sensitivity and precision.

In summary, a multi-signal sensing strategy with high sensitivity, simplicity, and efficient anti-interference ability for ALP detection was developed in this study for the first time. The strategy relied on the bifunctional CDs with unique peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence property. Further, a hydrogel device for ALP sensing was developed by integrating the system with hydrogel as the sensing phase carrier. Thus, utilizing the bifunctional CDs and the distinctive recognition between CDs and PPi, a multi-signal sensor integrated with colorimetry, fluorimetry, and smartphone modes was ingeniously developed for ALP sensing with robust performance. The developed strategy provides a reliable method for ALP detection and opens a new avenue for building multi-signal sensing systems with high accuracy and reliability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Start-Up Grant of Jining Medical University (600768001), and the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jining Medical University (601011001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2023.112172.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
